replies.author: @rpgadventurer32
replies.text: No need to cancel them, just you correct those who use them incorrectly in speech or in writing. These people don't use proper grammar because it contradicts their delusions. Plural pronouns can be used to describe "a person"; if that person's Sex is unknown or you just speak about a person in a generalize/impersonal terms. So there are grammatical cases where you do use plural third person pronouns to describe a singular person, but not like how they want it. These woke activists want to use them as a way to replace their own name and gender, as an identifiable measure in place of their own predetermined and immutable biological Sex. Plural third person pronouns should not be used for one person if you know who that person is and if you know that person is a male or a female, because once you do know, then the proper grammar would mean that you would need to use singular third person pronoun based on whether that person is a male or a female - "He" or "She" - in most cases except the ones I already mentioned above. You do not use third person pronouns in place of your own basic identifiable characteristics that exist in your ID. You don't say "my pronouns are" because they are already determined based on your Sex and that's how people will refer to you when speaking in third person about you. Usually people use "my name is..." or "I'm Ms or Mr..." etc. to introduce themselves in front of other people, though in most cases people would know what is your gender from visual identification alone so that means that usually - in almost all cases - people just use their private names to introduce themselves and nothing more. The Sex of the person determines what kind of singular third person pronoun is going to be used to describe them in third person in place of their private names, it's not determined by them in any way and they have no control over it. What these activists did is to distort the entire language to try and validate their non existent "non binary" gender identity. They want to force acceptance of that identity through the incorrect use of third person pronouns.

author: @ColdBrewMau
text:
Finally a breath of FRESH FUCKING AIR.

I have a left leaning coworker that tells me Taylor Sheridan is an “ok” writer purely for the fact that his shows are “virtue-signal-y” to which I replied “well, every other show out there on Disney, Hulu, Amazon, etc. does it so might as well deal with it when it comes back around to you”. 

Nobody likes getting preached to about ‘X’ when they’re watching media; they want to disconnect from the world for a while and enjoy entertainment. So as someone who’s also had to see all the virtue-signaling for YEARS, it’s funny seeing the other side get some thrown back at them. Does it fit in shows? No, neither should it be in Landman. But at this point it’s fair game and they/them should deal with it.

replies.author: @DeltaDanner
replies.text: As dark as it sounds, I had the same experience with my left leaning friends after Charlie Kirk’s assassination. Suddenly they were having to bite their tongues and walk on eggshells around me and my other right-leaning friends in the same way we’ve had to for years. The only difference is we were scared to talk about biology/psychology and they were scared to celebrate murder.

author: @vulcan3625
text:
There was a similar episode released this week on Shifting Gears (Tim Allen's new show with Kat Dennings). His old friend from high school stopped by his shop to catch up with him, and while they were chatting and joking about the past, his friend told him that he was gay and was married to a man. He wanted to know if Tim's character would come to his husband's show at his art gallery so he could meet him. Tim Allen's character is a Boomercon Republican who's portrayed as being a grumpy old man who's always complaining about communists and Democrats (so he'll make jokes about the left, and they'll make jokes about him and right leaning people). He also tells people to go fuck themselves, so people thought that he wasn't going to be okay with having a gay friend (he didn't care). His daughter Riley (Kat Dennings) was worried that he would end up saying something offensive to the gay dude, so she made him promise to be on his best behavior.

When he went to the art museum, he was actually nice to his buddy's husband, but the husband was super gay and kept insulting him and talking down to him the entire time he was there. His daughter pulled him to the side and asked him why he didn't tell the husband to go fuck himself? She wanted him to be on his best behavior and avoid being mean to people, but was okay with him telling someone off and defending himself if they were being mean to him. He said he chose not to do so because it wasn't the time and place for it. Even though the husband was rude as hell, he didn't want to cause a scene in their art gallery, and felt it was easier to just walk away. However, Riley, the daughter asked him if he was treating the guy differently because he was gay. If it was a straight person, he would've let them have it, but since the husband was gay, she felt like he was giving him a pass. He eventually told the husband off before he left, and the husband came by his shop the next day and apologized for being rude to him.

I was honestly surprised they even showed anything like that on tv because you're not allowed to show LGBT people in a negative light, but alot of people from that community are rude AF (and downright racist) to other people IRL. They get away with a lot of shit, and they always complain about other people being intolerant towards them, but they are extremely divisive and intolerant towards other people. I was glad that someone finally addressed it.

author: @KkOoTtFf
text: I grow up in one room with my sister and brother (it was 1990s/2000s Poland and most families had only two rooms apartments back then). So I had to learn to share "my safe space" with others. Because of that, I had no issues when I was crammed with 20 other guys in one room, when I was conscript in Polish Navy. Had no issues sharing my uni room with two other guys, when I was doing my studies. I had no issues having flatmates, when I was stealing british jobs, before brexit happened (I, hard working, paying taxes Pole am reason why UK decided to leave EU). This thought me to appriciate my own place even more. I'm thankful for my one bed flat (with annex converted to study). If I ever have to share it with someone, I'm flexible enough to survive and tolerate a lot, but I would never put any demands towards my potential roommate.

Brexit was long enough ago for you to have gotten over it.  

I am always annoyed by Europeans but when I meet someone from an individual country they are nice and represented the values of the culture of that nation.  Being European is basically like being an American tourist stereotype to us and the stereotype of Eurotrash comes from that early version of the EU ego of self importance like a US Tourist. I'm glad I met more Polish people today than yesterday. I hope you get over being European though.

author: @reefconvy7291
text:
When I started university, I tried to join the film club... and to my horror, HALF the people in that club were like this. The three people who were in charge of the club were like this: a lesbian who didn't call herself a girl, a gay man who didn't call himself a man, and a trans girl who just looked like a very camp gay man with a big black beard. We actually had to introduce ourselves with our names AND our pronouns. I had never been so uncomfortable in my life.

Needless to say, I never went back after the first night.

author: @Vin-v2p
text: I remember I used to play Fortnite when I was younger with this kid from South Carolina and when I knew him at the time he was 100% straight. Then this past summer he texted me out of nowhere and said he wanted to FaceTime. I called him the next day and we chatted for a bit and then someone comes in the room, telling him goodbye with a feminine voice. I ask him if he has a girlfriend, and then he comes out as gay right in my face in the person who said bye to him was his boyfriend. I didn’t even know how to feel.  Ultimately we ended all contact after that. That was the most awkward thing that has happened in my entire life.

author: @Shendue
text:
Technically using third person pronouns for a person of unspecified gender IS correct english language, tho.
It's not uniquely a statement of being genderfluid or whatever. For example if you are talking about someone you don't know the gender of, they/them is the most correct form.
Let's say a judge ruled someone guilty, you know about the trial but have no idea whether the judge is a man or a woman, you say "The judge was very harsh, apparently. They sentenced the suspect to lifetime prison".
I understand it's just a fun dialogue bit in a series, from a character who isn't necessarily an english professor, but still.

author: @MjollTheLioness-o4y
text:
I like Taylor Sheridan, but his shows absolutely are always better in the first half than the second. He has flubbed the endings to more than one of his stories, in my opinion. 

I absolutely hated the ending of 1923 which is a shame because it was a great series up until the last few episodes. Same with Yellowstone and 1883. For some reason, a lot of his shows end up having a lot of unneeded filler episodes and then rushed endings that need more fleshing out.

author: @NotOomay
text:
These people call anything that doesn't perfectly align with their EVER-changing, goal post moving narrative "toxic"  

When in reality, they are the most INSUFFERABLE humans to be around. To me it's a mixture of self-hatred and narcissism. They hate the whole world and so when they see someone else smiling and NORMAL its like silver to a vampire.

The only answer is the truth. The pure, unadulterated truth. Anything else is just a disservice to YOURSELF and to them.

Ainsley wasn't toxic in this scene. She was accepting and cordial. She pushed back on what didn't make sense to her, but not in any mean spirited way. It was a tame "I don't understand" way.

But of course, you won't get the purple haired, basement dwelling, cat litter shitting THEY people to admit that.

Which is why you don't try.  You just hit em with the truth and keep on truckin' Anything else is their problem. 

I'm a big fan of landman. This scene had me rolling. I had to rewind it and watch it back because like you said, it was a bit out of left field.

I DO agree on one point. It was a breathe of fresh air in that it didn't pander to the usual crowd. It was easier to stomach because it wasn't just outright fan-service.

But i'd rather it just wasn't in it at all either. It didn't really fill any required plotline. but damn did I enjoy the laugh.

Similar to people that only help others to make themselves feel better, then can't actually raise animals similar to how they can't raise themselves. 8/10 don't even care about what their animals needs because their feelings are what matter most. Similar to why people are appalled by an electric chair, yet find lethal injection to be okay. As long as they don't have to witness the pain or hear it, it's all okay and their feelings are in tact.
